old_claim.note: Maritime Insurance provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because maritime insurance supplied a preexisting risk-pricing application setting, while the scoped probability-theory node is the 1654 mathematical formalization from games of chance.

source_shape.source_locator: The MAA account identifies the Pascal-Fermat correspondence as solving the problem of points in a game of chance and describes risk management, actuarial science, and insurance as later consequences of the mathematics.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports insurance as a later application and impact of formal probability, not as the prerequisite that produced the 1654 formalization.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A maritime-insurance prerequisite would reverse the source-backed chronology by turning a later application domain into the causal input for the Pascal-Fermat gambling-problem mathematics.

ontology_before: Probability Theory depended directly on Maritime Insurance, implying marine insurance practice enabled the 1654 mathematical formalization.
ontology_after: Probability Theory is scoped to games-of-chance formalization, with insurance retained only as a later application in the node description.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: maritime_insurance is no longer a direct prerequisite for probability_theory.
would_reject_if: A stronger source showed the Pascal-Fermat correspondence was directly caused by maritime-insurance pricing practice rather than games of chance.
The node were rescoped to early marine-insurance premium calculation rather than mathematical probability theory.
A separate actuarial-probability node were added where maritime insurance is source-backed as the application domain.
short_reason: Maritime insurance is a later application/risk context, not a direct prerequisite for the 1654 probability formalization.
